_meta.description: Canonical source manifest for every numeric claim in Paywall Pilot. Each entry has a stable id plus source URL, publication date, dataset scope, domain, and evidence class.

evidence_classes.apple_docs: Official Apple documentation or App Review Guidelines
evidence_classes.large_scale_report: Published report from SDK provider or measurement platform covering 10K+ apps or $1B+ revenue
evidence_classes.vendor_blog: Blog post from SDK provider, may reference internal data without open methodology
evidence_classes.case_study: Single company result, not independently verified
evidence_classes.field_observation: Rejection patterns or production observations reported by developers/providers, not official Apple rule
evidence_classes.aggregate_study: Multi-company study with published methodology and sample size
evidence_classes.academic: Peer-reviewed academic paper or established behavioral economics finding
evidence_classes.platform_capability: Documented feature in StoreKit, App Store Connect, Google Play Billing, or third-party platform

domains.paywall: Current flagship domain for in-app subscription flows

status_values.legacy: Retained for history but not default

benchmarks.claim: Install → Trial global average: 10.9–11.2%
Install → Trial North America: 14.5%
Trial → Paid global: 25.6–27.8%
Trial → Paid Health & Fitness: 35.0–39.9%
Hard paywall D35 download-to-paid: 10.7%
Freemium D35 download-to-paid: 2.1%
Day 0 share of trial starts: 80–90%
Trial <4 days: 25.5% median conversion
Trial 5–9 days: 37.4% median conversion
Trial 17–32 days: 42.5% median conversion
Hard paywall RPI Day 14: $2.32
Hard paywall RPI Day 60: $3.09
Weekly plans: 55.5% of all revenue
H&F annual plans: 60.6% of revenue
Weekly + trial 12-month LTV: $49–$54
A/B test win rate — Localization: 62.3%
A/B test win rate — Visual/copy: 34.6%
Transaction abandon: 17% of total revenue (18 companies)
2 products vs 1: +61% conversion
3 products vs 2: +44% conversion
Top 10% apps capture 94.5% of revenue
Apps with 50+ experiments: 18.7x more revenue
Animated vs static paywalls: 2.9x conversion
Trial Timeline: +23% conversion, -55% complaints
Personalized paywalls: 15%+ lift
Toggle paywall rejections started mid-January 2026
benchmarks.scope: Confirmed App Store rejections, multiple independent reports

benchmarks.claim: ~16pt minimum font for pricing
benchmarks.scope: Developer rejection reports, not official Apple rule

benchmarks.claim: >5s delayed close button = high rejection risk
benchmarks.scope: Developer rejection reports, not official Apple rule

benchmarks.claim: 90% of subscriptions sell at full price
Trial subscribers retain 1.4–1.7x better than direct buyers
benchmarks.source: Adapty Free Trial vs Direct Purchase

benchmarks.claim: Post-close welcome offers: 10–15% ARPU lift
12-month retention — Annual: 44.1%, Monthly: 17.0%, Weekly: 3.4%
European apps charge 29–39% more than North American
Subscription UA spend grew 24% YoY; Android growing 4x faster than iOS
benchmarks.scope: 1.7B paid installs, 2,900 subscription apps, 13 categories, $2.1B UA spend

benchmarks.claim: Indian Subcontinent: 49% of net Android paid install growth; LATAM: 18%
Short Drama category YoY paid installs: +155%
Top 5 apps per category control >90% of UA spend
Google Play involuntary billing failures: ~31% of cancellations vs App Store 14%
3-day trials: 55.4% cancel Day 0; 7-day trials: 39.8% cancel Day 0
12-mo RLTV by region: NA $32, WE $25, IN/SEA $14
12-mo RLTV by tier: High $62.19, Mid $28.75, Low $10.69
H&F: 4.4x pricing variance across markets; install LTV $1.21 (highest of any category)
H&F first renewal rate 67.7%; 86.1% Day 0 conversion; secondary peak Days 4-7
Apple Guideline 3.1.2 toggle paywall reason: 'This design is confusing and may prevent users from understanding that they are committing to an auto-renewing subscription'
benchmarks.source: Apple App Store Review Guidelines + RevenueCat / Adapty rejection reports
benchmarks.scope: Apple-cited rejection reason, multiple developer reports

benchmarks.claim: Tversky & Kahneman framing effect — same problem framed gain vs loss produces preference reversal
benchmarks.source: Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1981). The framing of decisions and the psychology of choice. Science, 211(4481), 453-458

benchmarks.claim: Anderson & Simester $9 endings — same shirt at $39 outsold same shirt at $34 in catalog field experiment
benchmarks.source: Anderson, E. T., & Simester, D. I. (2003). Effects of $9 price endings on retail sales: Evidence from field experiments. Quantitative Marketing and Economics, 1, 93-110

benchmarks.claim: Thomas & Morwitz left-digit effect — consumers anchor on leftmost digit ($9.99 reads as 'nine-something' not '~$10')
benchmarks.source: Thomas, M., & Morwitz, V. (2005). Penny wise and pound foolish: The left-digit effect in price cognition. Journal of Consumer Research, 32(1), 54-64

benchmarks.claim: Ariely decoy effect — adding inferior $125 print-only option shifted preference toward $125 combo by 52pp (16% to 84%)
benchmarks.source: Ariely, D. (2008). Predictably Irrational, Ch. 1 — The Economist subscription experiment
benchmarks.caveat: Replication studies with real (non-hypothetical) money show weaker effect; treat as Operator Insight in mobile context

benchmarks.claim: Cialdini 7 principles — Social Proof and Authority test as most influential persuaders in mobile app contexts
benchmarks.source: Springer 2024 — Cialdini persuasion principles in mobile app contexts
benchmarks.scope: Multi-cultural study on mobile app installation/risk-taking

benchmarks.claim: Blinkist Trial Timeline paywall: +23% trial signups, -55% complaints, +4% trial retention, +1,200% notification opt-in (6%->74%)
Calm uses single-plan paywall: annual at £39.99, family at £40.99 for 6 accounts, monthly anchor strikethrough
benchmarks.source: Calm operator analysis (Kristen Berman) + Adapty Paywall Library + ScreensDesign

benchmarks.claim: Cal AI: $1.4M/month gross profit on 30M+ downloads, hard paywall + obsessive personalization model
Tinder blur-to-reveal paywall: ~8% upgrade conversion specifically to unblur 'who liked you'
benchmarks.source: StartupSpells Tinder Gold conversion analysis + Sub Club Podcast (Shawn Gong, Tinder)
benchmarks.scope: Operator disclosure on podcast + independent analysis

benchmarks.claim: ChatGPT pricing tiers (April 2026): Free, Go ($8 emerging-markets), Plus ($20, GPT-5.4), Pro ($200, GPT-5.4 Pro + Sora 2 Pro), Business, Enterprise
Flo: 70-screen onboarding, $6M/month subscription revenue from ~1M downloads/month, 60-80% purchases at first app use
Noom: 77-step onboarding (~7 min), 'personalized plan reserved' framing, 15-min countdown, 7-day trial on 2-month plan
benchmarks.source: Paddle Fix That Funnel + Retention.blog + ScreensDesign

benchmarks.claim: Strava: 30-day full-access trial without credit card; monthly $11.99 vs annual $79.99 anchoring
benchmarks.source: Top Fitness Paywalls + gadgetsandwearables coverage of 2025 Year-in-Sport paywalling

benchmarks.claim: Headspace: equal-prominence monthly and annual plans; 100% locked content with 1-2 free items per category retained as hook
benchmarks.source: Sub Club Podcast (Shreya Oswal & Keya Patel) + NamiML

benchmarks.claim: Kahneman & Tversky Prospect Theory — losses felt approximately 2x more painful than equivalent gains; foundational paper that won 2002 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economics
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. Econometrica, 47(2), 263-291

benchmarks.claim: Anchoring effect — even arbitrary numbers anchor subsequent estimates (spinner experiment: random number influenced estimates of unrelated facts)
benchmarks.source: Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1974). Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases. Science, 185(4157), 1124-1131
benchmarks.scope: Foundational paper introducing the heuristics and biases program

benchmarks.claim: System 1 / System 2 — fast intuitive decisions vs slow deliberate reasoning; 95%+ of decisions made by System 1 in under a few seconds
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow. Farrar, Straus and Giroux
benchmarks.scope: Best-selling synthesis of 40 years of Kahneman's research

benchmarks.claim: Endowment Effect — once people own something, they value it ~2x more than before they owned it (coffee mug experiment: $5 mug demanded $7 to sell, only $3 to buy)
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J. L., & Thaler, R. H. (1990). Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem. Journal of Political Economy, 98(6), 1325-1348

benchmarks.claim: Peak-End Rule — people judge experiences by emotional peak + ending, not by average intensity (colonoscopy study: longer mild-pain ending rated less painful than shorter sharp-pain)
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D., Fredrickson, B. L., Schreiber, C. A., & Redelmeier, D. A. (1993). When More Pain Is Preferred to Less: Adding a Better End. Psychological Science, 4(6), 401-405
benchmarks.scope: Lab + clinical studies on retrospective experience evaluation

benchmarks.claim: Default Effect / Status Quo Bias — organ donation rates 86% in opt-out countries vs 4% in opt-in countries (same population, only default flipped)
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J. L., & Thaler, R. H. (1991). Anomalies: The Endowment Effect, Loss Aversion, and Status Quo Bias. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 5(1), 193-206

benchmarks.claim: Mental Accounting — people categorize money into mental accounts (daily expenses vs entertainment vs investment); same dollar feels different by account assignment
benchmarks.source: Thaler, R. H. (1980, 1985, 1999). Toward a Positive Theory of Consumer Choice and subsequent papers. Built on Kahneman prospect theory foundation
benchmarks.scope: Foundational behavioral economics; explains per-day vs per-year framing differences

benchmarks.claim: WYSIATI (What You See Is All There Is) — System 1 makes confident judgments based only on visible information; unseen does not register as uncertain, registers as nonexistent
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow, Chapter 7
benchmarks.scope: Synthesis of decades of cognitive research

benchmarks.claim: Substitution Heuristic — when asked a hard question, the brain unconsciously substitutes an easier question and answers that instead
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow, Chapter 9
benchmarks.scope: Explains why social proof beats feature lists in conversion contexts

benchmarks.claim: Planning Fallacy — people consistently underestimate how long tasks will take and how hard they will be; best-case scenarios feel more likely than they are
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Intuitive Prediction: Biases and Corrective Procedures. TIMS Studies in Management Science, 12, 313-327
benchmarks.scope: Foundational paper on prediction biases; explains why trial users overestimate their cancellation discipline

benchmarks.claim: Hedonic Adaptation — people adapt to new conditions (positive or negative) faster than they expect; lottery winners and accident victims return toward baseline within months
benchmarks.source: Kahneman, D., Diener, E., & Schwarz, N. (Eds.) (1999). Well-Being: The Foundations of Hedonic Psychology. Russell Sage Foundation
benchmarks.scope: Edited volume synthesizing well-being research; explains reverse trial mechanics + habit-forming feature design

benchmarks.claim: Fogg Behavior Model — Behavior = Motivation × Ability × Trigger; all three must converge for behavior to occur; reduce ability friction to compensate for low motivation
benchmarks.source: Fogg, B. J. (2009). A behavior model for persuasive design. Proceedings of the 4th International Conference on Persuasive Technology, Article 40, 1-7. ACM
benchmarks.scope: 1,900+ academic publications reference the Fogg model; widely operationalized in product design

benchmarks.claim: Choice Overload — 6-jam supermarket display generated 30% purchase rate vs 24-jam display 3% (~10x conversion advantage); 3 experimental studies showing fewer choices increase satisfaction and conversion
benchmarks.source: Iyengar, S. S., & Lepper, M. R. (2000). When choice is demotivating: Can one desire too much of a good thing? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(6), 995-1006
benchmarks.scope: Foundational JPSP paper; effect direction validated; magnitude challenged in Scheibehenne et al 2010 meta-analysis (50+ studies)
benchmarks.caveat: Effect is context-dependent; meta-analysis found near-zero average. Direction valid; do not bank on 10x lift in mobile A/B.

benchmarks.claim: IKEA Effect — people value self-made products as much as expert-made products; effect dissipates if labor fails or product is destroyed (completion matters); 4 studies (IKEA boxes, origami, Lego)
benchmarks.source: Norton, M. I., Mochon, D., & Ariely, D. (2012). The IKEA effect: When labor leads to love. Journal of Consumer Psychology, 22(3), 453-460
benchmarks.scope: Replicated in conceptual replication studies; psychological ownership identified as mediator. Explains long-onboarding paywalls (Noom, Flo, Cal AI).

benchmarks.claim: Hyperbolic Discounting — people apply disproportionately steep discount to future rewards (present bias); creates dynamically inconsistent preferences; explains why weekly plans dominate over annual in mobile subscriptions
benchmarks.source: Laibson, D. (1997). Golden Eggs and Hyperbolic Discounting. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 112(2), 443-478
benchmarks.scope: Foundational behavioral-economics paper; decades of replication across savings, addiction, health behavior

benchmarks.claim: Goal-Gradient Effect — effort accelerates as users approach reward; café customers buy coffee more frequently near free-coffee threshold; bonus head-start (12-stamp card with 2 free) leads to faster completion than equivalent regular card
benchmarks.source: Kivetz, R., Urminsky, O., & Zheng, Y. (2006). The goal-gradient hypothesis resurrected: Purchase acceleration, illusionary goal progress, and customer retention. Journal of Marketing Research, 43(1), 39-58
benchmarks.scope: Multi-method field experiments + lab studies; robust replication base. Explains progress bars + bonus framing in onboarding.

benchmarks.claim: Negativity Bias — psychological impact of bad events outweighs equivalent good events across all domains (everyday events, major life events, relationships, social networks, learning, feedback); the self is more motivated to avoid bad self-definitions than to pursue good
benchmarks.source: Baumeister, R. F., Bratslavsky, E., Finkenauer, C., & Vohs, K. D. (2001). Bad is stronger than good. Review of General Psychology, 5(4), 323-370
benchmarks.scope: Comprehensive review; 10,000+ citations; most-cited paper in negativity bias literature

benchmarks.claim: Costly Signaling — buyers infer quality from observable costly signals; premium pricing signals quality when quality cannot be directly verified; foundational microeconomics
benchmarks.source: Spence, M. (1973). Job Market Signaling. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 87(3), 355-374
benchmarks.scope: Nobel Memorial Prize in Economics (2001); 14,759+ citations per Semantic Scholar. Explains 3x LTV uplift of high-priced apps (Adapty 2026)

benchmarks.claim: Psychological Reactance — when freedom to choose is threatened, people are motivated to restore freedom by doing the opposite; explains why fake urgency, hard re-prompting, and forced defaults backfire
benchmarks.source: Brehm, J. W. (1966). A Theory of Psychological Reactance. Academic Press
benchmarks.scope: 50+ years of replication across health, marketing, politics, education domains; reviewed in Steindl et al 2015 Springer Nature

benchmarks.claim: Sunk Cost Fallacy — people irrationally continue commitments based on past investment of time/money/effort even when continuing is no longer rational; strongest when investment is recent and visible
benchmarks.source: Arkes, H. R., & Blumer, C. (1985). The psychology of sunk cost. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 35(1), 124-140
benchmarks.scope: Foundational paper on sunk cost; widely accepted in academic literature. Explains commitment effect of long onboarding flows.

benchmarks.claim: Ego Depletion / Decision Fatigue — REPLICATION FAILED. Original 1998 study claimed self-control is a limited resource that depletes with use. Hagger et al 2016 (24 labs, 2,141 participants) and Vohs et al 2016 (36 labs, 3,531 participants) both found no effect. The simple resource-based model is no longer scientifically tenable.
benchmarks.source: Original: Baumeister, R. F., Bratslavsky, E., Muraven, M., & Tice, D. M. (1998). Ego Depletion: Is the Active Self a Limited Resource? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 74(5), 1252-1265. Replication failures: Hagger et al 2016 + Vohs et al 2016 multi-lab studies.
benchmarks.scope: Cautionary tale: do NOT cite as mechanism for design rules. Use Choice Overload (Iyengar 2000) + System 1 (Kahneman 2011) instead.
benchmarks.caveat: REPLICATION FAILED. Include only as cautionary tale; do not use as design justification.
